coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal method synthesized pH-dependent carbon dots for multistage anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Xiang
Zhao, Xia
Hu, Hao
Li, Zequan
Xiong, Wei
Wei, Yujiao
Gao, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Work to combat counterfeiting has always been crucial to defending the interests of the public. The usual anti-counterfeiting marks are now fundamental and easy to imitate. Therefore, it is more beneficial to anti-counterfeiting work to develop an anti-counterfeiting mark with more variations to make forgery more difficult. Due to its exceptional stability and fluorescence variability, carbon dots (CDs), a newly developed fluorescent material, offer a wide range of potential applications in anti-counterfeiting. However, there currently needs to be more CD applications in multi-level anti-counterfeiting, and additional issues include high cost and environmental contamination. Therefore, considering the problems of green environmental protection and cost, CDs with excellent green (530 nm) and blue (475 nm, 486 nm) luminescence properties were prepared by a one-step reaction of m-phenylenediamine and glucose. The average fluorescence lifespan is longer than 5 ns, and the optimal quantum yield can reach 37%. Due to the large number of protonated amino groups and surface carboxyl functional groups, the prepared carbon dots exhibit green and blue fluorescence emission modes under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. Based on this situation, we produced CD ink and successfully used it for multi-level anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH-dependence
Chromic fluorescence
Multi-level anti-counterfeiting
Information storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The internal and external characteristics of different carbon dots.
Acid and alkali: Fluorescence changes of carbon dots.
The emission mechanism of carbon points.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HTEM (Mosaic), and Particle size distribution (Mosaic) diagrams from 1-CDs to 5-CDs. (a) 1-CDs, (b) 2-CDs, (c) 3-CDs, (d) 4-CDs and (e) 5-CDs; (f) XRD patterns of 1-CDs to 5-CDs.
XPS C1s spectra and N1s spectra from 1-CDs to 5-CDs. (a) 1-CDs, (b) 2-CDs, (c) 3-CDs, (d) 4-CDs, and (e) 5-CDs; (f) The peak area of N–H and O–C=O from 1-CDs to 5-CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (a) 1-CDs, (b) 2-CDs, (c) 3-CDs, (d) 4-CDs, and (e) 5-CDs; (f) Normalized fluorescence spectra of 1-CDs to 5-CDs at 365 nm excitation wavelength.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum from 1-CDs to 5-CDs. (a) 1-CDs, 2-CDs, and 3-CDs; (b) 4-CDs and 5-CDs; (c) 1-CDs at pH 3 to pH 11; (d) Photograph of 1-CDs in different buffers of pH 3 to 11 under 365 nm UV light.
The fluorescence emission mechanism of CDs.

Color-changing fluorescent ink prepared by 1-CDs is used for anti-counterfeiting. (a) Anti-counterfeiting discoloration effect of color-changing ink; (b) Anti-counterfeiting and information storage; (c) The effect of fluorescence color-change after 10 days.
The average fluorescent lifetime from 1-CDs to 5-CDs.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal method synthesized pH-dependent carbon dots for multistage anti-counterfeiting

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, Guangxi, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Work to combat counterfeiting has always been crucial to defending the interests of the public. The usual anti-counterfeiting marks are now fundamental and easy to imitate. Therefore, it is more beneficial to anti-counterfeiting work to develop an anti-counterfeiting mark with more variations to make forgery more difficult. Due to its exceptional stability and fluorescence variability, carbon dots (CDs), a newly developed fluorescent material, offer a wide range of potential applications in anti-counterfeiting. However, there currently needs to be more CD applications in multi-level anti-counterfeiting, and additional issues include high cost and environmental contamination. Therefore, considering the problems of green environmental protection and cost, CDs with excellent green (530 nm) and blue (475 nm, 486 nm) luminescence properties were prepared by a one-step reaction of m-phenylenediamine and glucose. The average fluorescence lifespan is longer than 5 ns, and the optimal quantum yield can reach 37%. Due to the large number of protonated amino groups and surface carboxyl functional groups, the prepared carbon dots exhibit green and blue fluorescence emission modes under acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. Based on this situation, we produced CD ink and successfully used it for multi-level anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of human civilization, interests have begun to encroach on people's hearts. Many unscrupulous people dazed by interests have used fake and shoddy methods to obtain improper interests. Various forms of forgery have emerged, causing substantial economic losses to individuals and collectives. Although laws were enacted a long time ago to try to prevent this situation from continuing, some people still choose to take risks, driven by surprising interests. Therefore, the design of anti-counterfeiting marks has become essential to prevent this situation. Currently, anti-counterfeiting marks can be divided into three levels: first-level anti-counterfeiting, second-level anti-counterfeiting, and multi-level anti-counterfeiting [1]. Most of the traditional anti-counterfeiting materials are first-level anti-counterfeiting, which means that the anti-counterfeiting mark can change under the influence of external conditions; the secondary anti-counterfeiting can produce two changes, and the multi-level anti-counterfeiting can make three or more changes. First-level anti-counterfeiting and second-level anti-counterfeiting are relatively easy to imitate. Still, multi-level anti-counterfeiting has brought great difficulties to forgery due to its diversity of changes, which can effectively reduce the economic losses caused by forgery. Therefore, multi-level anti-counterfeiting should become a research focus.

The current anti-counterfeiting methods are mostly barcodes, two-dimensional codes, fluorescent tags, etc. Among them, fluorescence tags will show the fluorescence effect under the excitation of special band light, and its anti-counterfeiting product is relatively reasonable and widely used. Traditional fluorescent materials can be roughly divided into organic and inorganic fluorescent materials. Nowadays, the materials used in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting are mainly inorganic phosphors prepared by various methods, such as perovskite [2–5] and rare earth elements [6–8]. Although rare earth resources are relatively wealthy, rare earth materials belonging to non-renewable resources are urgently needed to find alternative products. Organic fluorescent materials can be divided into polymer organic fluorescent materials and small molecule organic fluorescent materials. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) as organic small molecule fluorescent materials have attracted wide attention due to their excellent photoluminescence (PL) effect, high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, cheaply and readily available raw materials, low preparation cost, and low toxicity. Since its successful preparation, it has been widely used in biological imaging [9–13], fluorescent nanosensors [14–18], photocatalysts [19–22], optoelectronic devices [23–27], and anti-counterfeiting [28–34].

CDs can exhibit fluorescence emission effects of various colors under the excitation of ultraviolet light, so they have broad development prospects in anti-counterfeiting. The change in fluorescence emission of CDs is affected by excitation wavelength, fluorescence lifetime, solvent type, and other physical and chemical factors, which is also why it can produce the multi-level anti-counterfeiting effect. The excitation-dependent CDs emit different colors of fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths [35–37], thereby achieving multi-level anti-counterfeiting effects; the CDs affected by excitation wavelength and fluorescence lifetime are multi-level anti-counterfeiting formed by the change of phosphorescence with time after the excitation light is turned off [38–40]; solvent type is to change the surface defects of CDs to change its emission wavelength to achieve the multi-level anti-counterfeiting effect [41–44]. At the same time, some CDs also have the characteristics of fluorescence emission at different temperatures, different concentrations, and different pressures [45–47]. Recently, Song et al. used the calcination method to prepare CDs @ ZnAl2O4 powder with L-lysine as a carbon source and mixed aluminum nitrate and zinc nitrate to make it have fluorescence and phosphorescence effects [39]. Bai et al. prepared polarity-dependent color-changing fluorescent CDs hydrothermally using 2, 3-dichloro-5, 6-dicyano-p-benzoquinone, and resorcinol [41]. Fu et al. prepared an anti-counterfeiting ink that can effectively inhibit fluorescence quenching and change color with the change of environmental pH value by dispersing silicon-carbon hybrid points into carboxymethyl cellulose binder [42]. These provide a strong foundation for the application of CDs in multi-level anti-counterfeiting, but there are still problems, such as environmental pollution, high cost, and cumbersome operation.

Therefore, in order to reduce pollution, reduce cost, simplify the preparation process, and obtain a good fluorescence conversion effect, glucose, and m-phenylenediamine (mPD), which are relatively easy to get and less polluting to the environment, were selected as raw materials to prepare pH-dependent fluorescent CDs by one-step hydrothermal method. They were optically characterized, and it was found that all CDs had high fluorescence intensity. The fluorescent CDs designed under acidic conditions exhibit green fluorescence emission, while those prepared under alkaline conditions exhibit blue fluorescence emission. At the same time, as the pH value of the solvent in which the CDs are located changes from low to high, the fluorescence will also change from green to blue. Various characterization methods show that the change in CDs emission behavior is not affected by the carbon nucleus and size but is caused by the change of surface functional groups. Based on this change in emission behavior, a simple pH-responsive fluorescent ink coating was prepared for multi-level anti-counterfeiting.

Glucose, mPD, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, trisodium phosphate, and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with different pH values was purchased from Haibiao Technology Co., Ltd. (Fuzhou, China). All reagents were used without further purification. The ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ * cm (25 °C) was used in the experiment.

Firstly, 0.2 g glucose and 0.4 g mPD were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water, 400 uL phosphoric acid was added, and the initial solution was obtained by violently stirring until completely dissolved. Subsequently, the initial solution was transferred to a PTFE stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C in an oil bath for 30 min. The dark brown solution was obtained after natural cooling to room temperature. Large particles were filtered using an injection filter with a pore size of 0.22 um and dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da to remove unreacted small molecules. At the same time, acetic acid was used instead of phosphoric acid to prepare 2-CDs, without adding any acid; only glucose and m PD were used to prepare 3-CDs. In addition, to study the PL emission properties of CDs under alkaline conditions, we replaced 400 μL phosphoric acid with 1.0 g Na3PO4 and 0.8 g Na OH, respectively, and prepared 4-CD and 5-CD by the same synthesis method as 1-CDs. The dialyzed solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained at 300 kV using FEI TECNAI G2 F30 field emission transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA). XRD analysis was performed using Rigaku D/MAX 2500 V (Rigaku, Japan). Raman spectroscopy was measured using an inVia Reflex instrument (Renishaw, UK) with a laser wavelength of 718 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was performed using the Nicolet iS 50 spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected at the ESCALAB 250 xi + Electron Spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). The surface charge of the synthesized CDs was measured using the NanoBrook Omni instrument (Brookhaven, USA); the laser was a 40 mW optically pumped semiconductor red laser. The fluorescence spectrum was measured by the F-320 spectrophotometer (Gangdong, China), and the excitation and emission slits were set to 5 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples were analyzed by UV-2540 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The FL3C-111 TCSPC instrument system (HORIBA, Japan) was used to measure the time-resolved fluorescence decay curve under 410 nm excitation. The sample’s absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured by the OmniFluo990 LSP instrument (China, Zolix) and an integrating sphere. The CDs powder was dissolved in an ethanol solution with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. All measurements were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The internal and external characteristics of different carbon dots.
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the microscopic morphology of CDs, we analyzed the TEM, HTEM, and particle size distribution of the prepared 1-CDs to 5-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 a–e, from which we can observe finely dispersed carbon nanodots. The analysis of its particle size indicates that the average particle sizes of 1-CDs to 5-CDs are 7.69 nm, 9.9 nm, 10.21 nm, 21.67 nm, and 11.55 nm, respectively. There is a clear gap between the particle sizes of each CDs, and the particle size of CDs prepared under alkaline conditions is more significant than that of CDs designed under acidic and neutral conditions. HTEM shows that 1-CDs to 5-CDs have the same crystal plane spacing of 0.34 nm, consistent with the graphite (002) lattice plane represented by 21° in Fig. 1f. This indicates the successful preparation of CDs with similar carbon core structures. Raman spectra (Fig. S1) show that 1-CDs and 5-CDs have distinct D and G peaks at 1582 cm−1 and 1337 cm−1. Their ID/IG ratios were 0.6, 0.61, 0.9, 0.86, and 1.1, respectively, reflecting a good degree of graphitization. It can be inferred that the higher the degree of graphitization of CDs, the denser the carbon nucleus and the smaller the particle size.

Then we analyzed the carbonized structures of five CDs by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. S2, including the stretching vibrations of O–H (3421 cm−1), N–H (3213 cm−1) and C=O (1670 cm−1), the bending vibrations of C–H (2914 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1) and C=C/C=N (1607 cm−1) and C–N (1396 cm−1), indicating the presence of O and N-containing groups. Because 2-CDs were prepared under the condition of adding acetic acid, no heteroatoms were introduced, and the functional groups were consistent with 3-CDs. Phosphoric acid and sodium phosphate synthesize CDs, introduce new elements, and form new functional groups. Therefore, 1-CDs and 4-CDs showed P-O-R (R-aromatic structure) and P-O–H structure [48–50] at 1060 cm−1 and 937 cm−1, which were not originally present in other samples, but because glucose itself has more C-O structure, in the spectrum, the flexural vibration peak of C-O also occurs around 1060 cm−1 [51,52], so all samples have a peak here. It is worth noting that compared with 1-CDs to 3-CDs, the characteristic absorption peaks of 4-cd and 5-cd at 3213 cm−1 are not prominent. In contrast, the peaks at 1670 cm−1 are relatively prominent, indicating that the N–H stretching vibration of CDs prepared in the alkaline environment is small and the C=O stretching vibration is large. Therefore, it can be inferred that the acid-base conditions of CDs preparation will affect the change of surface functional groups; that is, the surface of CDs prepared under acidic conditions contains more N functional groups, while the surface of CDs prepared under alkaline conditions contains more carboxyl functional groups.

To verify the above inference and further explore the changes in functional groups and elements, we performed XPS analysis on CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a-f and Fig. S3. From the survey spectra, we can easily know the binding energy peaks of CDs are C1s (284.8 eV), O1s (399 eV), N1s (532 eV), P1s (191 eV) and P2p (133 eV). The C1s from 1-CDs to 3-CDs can be decomposed into four peaks: C=C (284.5 eV), C-P/C-N (285 eV), C–O (286 eV), O–C=O (288 eV). N1s can be decomposed into three peaks: C–N=C (398 eV), N–(C) 3 (399 eV), N–H (400 eV); O1s can be decomposed into three peaks: C=O (530 eV), C–OH (531 eV), and C-O-C (532 eV). In addition to the above peaks, the O1s of 4-CDs and 5-CDs have an additional peak of O–C=O (535.5 eV). At the same time, from the element content and peak area (Fig. 2f), it can be seen that the N–H functional group content from 1-CDs to 3-CDs is much larger than that of 4-CDs and 5-CDs. Still, the O–C=O functional group content of 4-CDs and 5-CDs is greater than that of 1-CDs to 3-CDs. The N–H functional group content from 1-CDs to 3-CDs is greater than the O–C=O functional group content, while 4-CDs and 5-CDs are the opposite; the N–H functional group content is less than the O–C=O functional group content. This is consistent with the change of functional groups found by FT-IR.

Then, we characterize the Zeta potential of CDs. As shown in Table S1, the Zeta potentials of 1-CDs to 5-CDs are 17.28 eV, 9.8 eV, 15.42 eV, −16.04 eV, and −13.19 eV, respectively. It can be observed that 1-CDs through 3-CDs are positively charged. Combining the results of XPS and FT-IR, it can be seen that this is due to the dominance of their protonated amino groups. The negative charge state of 4-CDs and 5-CDs is caused by the dominance of free carboxyl groups on their surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid and alkali: Fluorescence changes of carbon dots.
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute quantum yields of 1-CDs to 5-CDs were 37 %, 9.2 %, 8.7 %, 1.5 %, and 3.7 %, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of the five CDs solutions prepared at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 3 a–f. It can be seen from Fig. 3a–c that the fluorescence emission intensity increases first and then decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is 460 nm, the 1-CDs to 3-CDs samples reach the maximum emission peak, and the peak is concentrated at about 530 nm, indicating their green emission characteristics. For these three CDs samples, although the reaction conditions are different, their emission spectra are similar, showing that the various acidic components and the introduction of phosphorus do not affect the effect of green fluorescence emission, and the luminescence characteristics of CDs prepared without acid and alkali components are similar to those prepared under acidic conditions. At the same time, the emission peaks of the three CDs did not change with the excitation wavelength, showing emission behavior independent of the excitation wavelength. In contrast, 4-CDs and 5-CDs reached the most substantial emission peaks at about 486 nm and 475 nm under 380 nm and 360 nm excitation, respectively, and showed emission-dependent behavior related to excitation wavelength (Fig. 3d, e). Notably, 4-CDs and 5-CDs showed differences in the most substantial emission peaks. Compared with 5-CDs, 4-CDs showed an apparent redshift, possibly due to the introduction of the P element [48]. As shown in Fig. 3f, the normalized diagram at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm clearly indicates that the emission wavelength is blue-shifted from 531 nm under acidic conditions to 485 nm under alkaline conditions.

It can be seen that the CDs prepared by the same precursor under different acid-base conditions have different fluorescence colors. From the characterization of the fundamental properties of CDs, we can know that because their lattice morphology, crystallinity, and so on are relatively close, and it does not show the discoloration trend related to the size effect [53,54], it can be speculated that the discoloration principle is not related to the carbon nucleus. It is worth noting that the N, O element content and related functional groups of CDs prepared under different acid and alkali conditions have a significant gap, and the defects related to its surface functional groups may be the key to the change of fluorescence color.

Then we observe the change of the absorption band by the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 4 a–c). The UV absorption of all prepared CDs samples shows different absorption bands centered at 280 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 domain of aromatic C=C and C=N. At the same time, 1-CDs to 3-CDs have two broad absorption bands near 375 nm and 450 nm, while the wide absorption bands of 4-CDs and 5-CDs at 450 nm disappear. It is not difficult to speculate that the disappearance of this absorption band is the key to the change in fluorescence properties. The absorption band near 375 nm may be related to the defect state in blue CDs. The wide absorption band of green CDs in the long wavelength region of about 450 nm indicates that N atom doping leads to new defect states in the π-π* gap during the formation of CDs nanostructures. Therefore, it can be inferred that the green emission and non-excitation-dependent behavior of CDs are caused by the low energy band generated by the uniform N-related defect states, while the large energy band gap generated by the O-related defect states caused by the surface carboxylated functional groups is the main reason for the blue emission and excitation-dependent behavior of CDs.

It was observed that the change of the absorption band was affected by the pH value, so the UV–visible absorption spectra of 1-CDs solutions with different pH values were detected, as shown in Fig. 4c. We found that the absorption bands at 280 nm and 375 nm do not change with the increase of pH value. The wide absorption band at 450 nm gradually weakened and disappeared when the pH value was greater than 8. This change indicates that the N-related defects gradually disappear with the increase of pH, and the decrease of N content of CDs in an alkaline environment is consistent with the results obtained by XPS. It also confirms the Zeta potential results: surface carboxyl groups dominate CDs under alkaline conditions. These findings further confirm that pH-dependent PL performance is related to the evolution of defect states.

As shown in Fig. 4d, we used PBS buffer with different pH to configure 1-CDs into CDs solution with pH 3–11. It can be seen from the figure that with the increase of pH from 3 to 11, the PL emission of CDs solution will have a transition process from green to blue-green, pH 3–7 emits green light, and pH 8–11 begins to emit blue-green light. This indicates that the change in solution pH will affect the fluorescence emission of CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, we detected the fluorescence changes of 1-CDs solutions with different pH values at excitation wavelengths of 360 nm and 440 nm. It can be seen from Fig. S4a that two different excitation wavelengths make the PL intensity of CDs show different trends with the change in pH. The PL intensity under 360 nm excitation increases and reaches the maximum at pH 10. When the excitation wavelength is 440 nm, the luminescence intensity decreases, and the luminescence intensity is the highest when the pH is 5. At the same time, as shown in Fig. S4b, the PL properties of 1-CDs gradually changed with the increase of pH value under 360 nm excitation. As the pH value increases from 3 to 7, the emission peak position blue shifts from 523 nm to 501 nm. In contrast, the emission peak obtained under 440 nm excitation is almost unaffected by pH changes (Fig. S4c) and always exhibits green emission. This may be due to the long excitation wavelength, insufficient to support the energy of the π-π* transition. The electrons can only be excited to the N-related defect state and only show green light emission. As the pH value increases, the N-related defects gradually decrease, resulting in a decrease in their intensity.

The fluorescence lifetimes of five CDs were measured. The decay curves of the samples were fitted by the quadratic fitting method shown in Formula (1). The Formula (2) calculated the samples' fluorescence lifetimes. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence lifetimes of 1-CDs to 5-CDs were 5.92 ns, 5.72 ns, 5.47 ns, 4.65 ns, and 5.08 ns, respectively. The formula is as follows:
(1)
y
( t )
=
A 1
e x p

A 1

)

Among them, A1 and A2 are the contribution fractions of the fluorescence decay, τ1, and τ2 are the corresponding decay time, and τaverage refers to the average lifetime. The specific parameters are shown in Fig. S5a-e. It can be seen from Fig. S5f that the prepared CDs have two obvious fluorescence emission sources. The A1 component of 1-CDs to 3-CDs is significantly higher than the A2 component. In comparison, the A2 component of 4-CDs and 5-CDs is higher than the A1 component, which also explains the fluorescence emission changes of CDs on the other hand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The emission mechanism of carbon points.
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 , based on the above results and previous studies, we propose a mechanistic model to understand the PL emission of the CDs we prepared [48]. Under alkaline conditions, the O-containing groups on the sp2-hybrid carbon will form local defects and produce different energy levels in the π-π* gap. The band gap caused by O-related defect states caused by O-containing functional groups dominates due to surface carboxyl functional groups. The excitation light of different excitation wavelengths excites electrons from the ground state (π) to different vibrational levels of the excited state (π*). Then, most of the excited electrons are captured by O-related defect states. Due to the non-radiative transition between different energy levels within the O-related defects, the fluorescence of each energy level is different, resulting in excitation-dependent blue light emission. At the same time, the maximum emission of 4-CDs has a significant red shift compared with 5-CDs. This is due to the P element in the 4-CDs producing a P element-related defect on the HOMO of the CDs. Therefore, for 4-CDs, the trapped electrons undergo P-related defect states before and after the radiation of O-related defect states, resulting in different PL behaviors. The N element generally causes the PL emission of CDs to show a redshift trend [55,56]; this is because the electron absorption and supply capacity of functional groups will affect the defect states below the π* state. Protonated amino groups can supply electrons, increasing the CDs’ electron density, resulting in a narrower optical band gap. Therefore, the N-related defect state caused by the protonated amino group in CDs prepared under acidic conditions is the intrinsic reason for the narrowing of the energy gap, leading to CDs’ green PL emission.

When they are excited for blue CDs, the electrons on the π orbital of the luminescent conjugated unit are excited to the π* orbital. Then, some electrons in the excited state may relax to the energy level of the corresponding O-related defect state. Finally, recombine with the holes in the π orbital or radiate to the P-related defect state, resulting in blue light emission. At the same time, the electrons of other excited states relax to N-related defect states and finally return to the π orbital through radiative transition, resulting in green light emission. However, the longer excitation wavelength will promote the capture of excited electrons by N-related defect states. Therefore, when the excitation wavelength gradually increases, more electrons are captured by N-related defect states, so the PL emission of CDs gradually transforms into green emission. When the wavelength reaches 440 nm, it only shows green emission.

The prepared 1-CDs were dissolved in water and continuously irradiated with a 395 nm ultraviolet lamp for 5 h (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity was measured. It was found that the fluorescence intensity generally decreased with the increase in irradiation time. Still, the downward trend gradually slowed, indicating that although it had a certain ultraviolet-quenching effect, it was still relatively stable. Due to the aggregation effect of small molecules, the dried CDs will be affected by temperature when dissolved into the solvent (Fig. S7). When the temperature is low, the solubility of CDs powder in water is small, and the fluorescence intensity is not high. When we heat it at 60 °C for some time, it can restore a higher fluorescence intensity. At the same time, the fluorescence color transformation in different acid-base environments shows that 1-CDs aqueous solution can be prepared into anti-counterfeiting ink, which makes it possible to manufacture optical anti-counterfeiting labels and encrypt and store text information. We use 1-CDs dissolved in deionized water to prepare carbon dot ink and write it on filter paper, as shown in Fig. 6 a. It shows light brown-yellow under natural light and green under 365 nm fluorescence irradiation. After spraying an alkaline solution (such as sodium hydroxide) on its surface, the imprinting becomes a cyan luminescence effect, and we can change back to green fluorescence emission after spraying the diluted acetic acid solution on it again. This excellent discoloration effect has a strong recognition ability. Then we compare 1-CDs and ordinary fluorescent CDs by coating them at different positions in the digital table. As shown in Fig. 6b, since ordinary CDs do not change with the change in environmental acidity and alkalinity, information can be read under alkaline and closed under acidic conditions. When we apply the 1-CDs ink to the two halves of the tai chi icon, the right side does not do other processing, and the left side is sprayed with alkaline and acidic solutions, we can see the obvious pairs as shown in Fig. 6c, and it still has good fluorescence effect and discoloration ability after 10 days of storage. Finally, we briefly show some articles about the application of CDs in anti-counterfeiting in the past few years (Table S2); we can find that our work is simple and easy and can achieve better performance. This work is expected to contribute to the development of multi-level anti-counterfeiting.

In this paper, CDs with the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 37 % were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine and glucose, and the CDs have excellent fluorescence intensity, good stability, and a long fluorescence lifetime of about 5 ns. At the same time, the fluorescence color of the CDs under acid and alkali conditions is not caused by the carbon core and size effect but due to the large number of functional groups containing N and O on the surface of the CDs. pH value can induce the fluorescence color to change by affecting the dominant position of the functional groups of the CDs. Based on this principle, applying fluorescent ink with multi-level anti-counterfeiting characteristics was tried, and good results were obtained.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Key R&D projects in Guangxi (AB21196033), and by Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AD23026201), and by Key R&D projects in Guangxi (2021AB06038), and by Interdisciplinary Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. 2022JCC017), Industry-university Research Cooperation Fund of Guangxi Jinding Furniture Group Co., Ltd & Guangxi University (20200820).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of multicolor photoluminescent polymer carbon dots with surface-state energy gap-controlled emission
